chain_edges.notes: SGLang CVE-2026-5760 is a concrete CVSS 8+ LLM-integration CVE instance feeding the broader Patch-Tuesday-regular thesis into GGUF-specific gating.
Signed model-cards and chat_template verification become artifacts a registry standard would distribute alongside agent skills and permissions.
Native 1-bit GGUF derivatives (Bonsai-8B-gguf) expand the GGUF artifact surface that hubs must then gate behind signed model-cards.
5-pole hyperscaler-lab lock-in raises demand for portable agent artifacts and registry standards alongside MCP.
Backend portability becoming a hard requirement reinforces MCP / registry surface conventions like headless-preferred metadata.
Closed-weight frontier (Qwen3.6-Max API-only) reinforces local-first/cloud-overflow split: open weights handle utility, cloud handles frontier.
Closed-weight frontier crystallization is precondition for hyperscaler-frontier-lab exclusive alliance pattern.
1-bit native training cements open-weight production tier ceiling at edge-class, supporting the split's open side.

relations.notes: GGUF SSTI gating is a narrower, GGUF-specific instance of the broader LLM-integration CVE category becoming a Patch Tuesday regular.
Both name new agent/model supply-chain attack categories (GGUF artifacts vs CI secret exfiltration) without overlapping outcome spaces.
Both center on GGUF artifacts but address independent facets: native 1-bit training recipe versus model-load SSTI gating.
Local-first coding agents rely on GGUF distribution; supply-chain gating is a parallel prerequisite, not a stronger relation.
Agent Registry distributes agent artifacts; model-card/chat_template signing is a parallel registry-shaped outcome for model artifacts.
Closed-weight frontier (focal) and exclusive hyperscaler-frontier-lab alliance (sibling) are co-occurring facets of proprietary consolidation.
Independent facets of the portability story: alliance lock-in vs registry standard for agent artifacts.
Both push MCP / registry surfaces as the portability layer, but along different axes (chip/cloud vs headless service preference).
Same-day siblings on AI supply-chain structure: closed hyperscaler alliances vs open-weight GGUF distribution risks.
Both describe the open/closed split: focal at model-tier level, sibling at deployment-stack level. Compatible facets.
Both presume an open-weight ecosystem; GGUF supply-chain risks are downstream of the open-weight production tier focal posits.
1-bit native training reinforces the open-weight production tier (35B-A3B/Bonsai/Llama) the focal pins as the open ceiling.
